projects.title: Accessible Web Calculator
projects.tagline: A calculator that takes keyboard support, percentage maths, and 0.1 + 0.2 more seriously than it probably needed to.
projects.story: This began as a small calculator and immediately turned into a negotiation with percentages, keyboard shortcuts, history, memory, and JavaScript arithmetic. I pair-programmed it with Claude, then kept tightening the edge cases until the simple surface had a reasonably serious foundation. It is a useful reminder that even tiny products contain opinions — especially the percent button.
capabilities: Basic operations with operator precedence and nested parentheses
Scientific actions: square, square root, reciprocal, sign toggle, percent
50-item history with timestamps and click-to-reuse results
Memory controls: MC, MR, M+, M-
Light/dark theme with system preference detection
Full keyboard navigation, ARIA labels, screen-reader friendly
Precision-safe arithmetic to avoid floating-point errors

projects.title: A11y Screenshot Justifier
projects.tagline: A screenshot triage tool that points at likely accessibility problems — then reminds you it is not an auditor.
projects.story: I built this for the awkward moment when I only had a screenshot but still wanted to begin an accessibility review. It flags likely contrast, focus, and labelling issues, then pairs each guess with a confidence level and a proper verification step. The important feature is the disclaimer: it speeds up a first pass; it does not replace people, source inspection, or real accessibility tools.
capabilities: Drag-and-drop PNG/JPG screenshot upload, analyzed heuristically
Switches between web UI and Windows desktop UI targets
Flags likely contrast, missing focus indicators, icon-only buttons, keyboard/focus-order risks
Structured findings with confidence ratings and verification steps
Exports JSON and copy-paste-ready bug justifications
Runs as an Electron desktop app with a Vite renderer

projects.title: ADO Design Review Bot
projects.tagline: A first-pass PR reviewer that asks whether the code and the Figma file are still on speaking terms.
projects.story: I built this to make the first ten minutes of an Azure DevOps design review less manual. The bot gathers PR context, reads Figma signals, runs a vision pass, and produces a draft review instead of pretending it should post final judgement unsupervised. Building a reviewer with AI involved a lot of reviewing the reviewer — mostly prompts, evidence, and knowing when it should simply say it is unsure.
capabilities: Runs a design review for an ADO pull request from CLI or MCP server
Accepts PR id plus repo/project/org overrides or ADO pipeline env vars
Pulls PR context and compares code changes with Figma-derived design info
Exposes an MCP server entrypoint for agent-based usage
Supports dry-run output instead of posting back to the PR
projects.run_cmd: npm install && npm run build && npm run start:mcp

projects.title: GifGen
projects.tagline: A small utility born from being tired of looking up the same FFmpeg flags.
projects.story: I kept converting product recordings into lightweight web assets and kept forgetting the same FFmpeg options. So I made a CLI with practical presets, batch support, and a tiny upload API. Claude helped shape the command structure; my main contribution was repeatedly asking, “Can this have one fewer flag?” It is not glamorous, but it has saved me from several browser tabs.
capabilities: Converts a single video into GIF, WebP, or both
Presets: web-optimized, high-quality, thumbnail, hero, feature-demo, gallery
Custom FPS, dimensions, colors, dithering, lossy compression, trim
Batch converts a directory of videos, including recursive runs
Reads a batch JSON config for repeatable conversions
Small Express upload API with job tracking and FFmpeg health checks

projects.title: FHL Agent Design
projects.tagline: A one-page agent UX concept built more like a visual essay than an application.
projects.story: This was a quick way to make an agent-design argument tangible. The page has one route, a handful of sections, and far more product storytelling than application logic — deliberately. I pair-programmed the build with Claude and resisted the urge to turn a visual concept into an architecture diagram. Sometimes the useful prototype is simply the one people can scroll through and discuss.
capabilities: Hero + narrative sections for the design story
Dedicated shift / ideas / workflow content sections
Source/reference section at the end
Single-page scroll experience

projects.title: System Messages
projects.tagline: A documentation prototype for the quiet Copilot messages that appear when no one is chatting.
projects.story: System messages are small, easy to overlook, and surprisingly good at creating confusion. I made this documentation-style prototype to organise their anatomy, behaviour, scenarios, and validation in one place. There is very little app logic because the information architecture is the product. It is essentially a design explanation that learned enough Next.js to become clickable.
capabilities: Classification framework for system messages
Sectioned docs layout with sidebar and TOC
Anatomy and content guidelines sections
Behavior/scenario/validation reference sections
Single-page content navigation

projects.title: Agent Canvas
projects.tagline: A Figma-ish canvas for specifying how an agent moves, decides, responds, and gets judged.
projects.problem: Agent behaviour usually disappears into prompts, configuration, and orchestration code. That makes the most important product decisions hard to see, critique, or hand to someone else.
projects.portfolio_signal: This is my clearest attempt to treat agent behaviour as designed material. The interface is a prototype, but the underlying model — steps, decisions, responses, and weighted quality criteria — is the part I wanted to make discussable.
evidence: Three sample scenarios model onboarding, file analysis, and error recovery as connected five-step flows.
Four step types — trigger, action, decision, and response — give the canvas a small but coherent behaviour vocabulary.
Quality criteria carry must, should, and nice-to-have weights rather than sitting in a separate checklist.
Preview mode replays a simulated conversation and cross-highlights the active canvas node.
limitations: The preview currently replays one hard-coded scenario rather than compiling the flow being edited.
Edits live only in local component state; refresh the page and they are gone.
Dragging, adding steps, and some toolbar controls are visual promises, not implemented tools.
There is no agent backend, persistence layer, automated test suite, or deployment yet.
projects.learning: A prototype can be useful before it is technically complete if it gives a team better nouns to argue with. The canvas made the missing pieces obvious too: persistence matters, but compiling an editable flow into a real preview matters more.
projects.story:
I kept running into the same awkward handoff: we could draw a user flow, write a prompt, or make a prototype, but none of those quite described how an agent should behave turn by turn. So I sketched a tool that borrows the spatial confidence of Figma without pretending a conversation is just a flowchart.

Each scenario is built from typed steps. Select one and the inspector shows its instruction, output, and quality criteria. Switch to Preview and the same structure becomes a small conversation replay, with the active message and canvas node lighting up together.

Some of that is deliberate theatre. The drag handles do not yet drag, and the preview is simulated. I built enough of the interaction to test the product idea first: can an agent spec feel tangible enough for a designer, PM, and engineer to point at the same thing?
capabilities: Switch between editable flow and conversation preview modes
Browse three sample agent scenarios
Inspect typed trigger, action, decision, and response steps
Edit step instructions and toggle weighted quality criteria
Replay a simulated conversation with active-step highlighting
Pan and zoom around the canvas

projects.title: Behavior Diff
projects.tagline: A before-and-after viewer for the dangerous little phrase: “I only changed one prompt.”
projects.problem: A one-line change to a prompt, policy, or context can quietly rewrite an agent's whole response. Reading two walls of text side by side is a poor way to understand what actually moved.
projects.portfolio_signal: This is a small prototype with a strong rule: change one variable at a time. That constraint turns a flashy before-and-after demo into something closer to an explainable behaviour experiment.
evidence: Three scenarios isolate a quality-bar change, a new VIP constraint, and an escalated customer input.
A custom longest-common-subsequence engine computes word-level additions, removals, and unchanged spans.
Split and unified views share one diff result, with line alignment and empty rows keeping comparisons legible.
Change statistics, configuration deltas, and a simulated rerun state complete the intended evaluation loop.
limitations: The outputs are authored fixtures; the rerun button does not call a live model.
The scenarios cover one customer-support domain rather than a broader behaviour benchmark.
Configuration is visible but not editable, so new experiments still require code changes.
There is no backend, persistence, automated test suite, or committed deployment.
projects.learning: If I change three things and compare two outputs, I learn almost nothing. The useful design decision was not the red-and-green diff; it was forcing every scenario to state the single variable that changed.
projects.story:
Agent changes can look tiny in configuration and enormous in the answer. I wanted the equivalent of a code diff for behaviour: not a score that says better or worse, but a view that shows where the response expanded, disappeared, or changed its mind.

The prototype ships with three controlled scenarios. One tightens the quality bar, one adds a VIP constraint, and one changes only the incoming message. A custom word-level diff engine feeds both split and unified views, so the comparison stays consistent whichever reading mode feels clearer.

The rerun is simulated for now. That is an important limitation, but it also kept the prototype honest: I could concentrate on the comparison grammar before spending time on an evaluation backend. The next real test is whether the same interface survives live, messy, non-deterministic runs.
capabilities: Switch between three one-variable scenarios
Compare outputs in split or unified views
Inspect word-level additions, removals, and unchanged text
Read aggregate change statistics
See the exact configuration, constraint, or input delta
Replay a simulated before-and-after run

projects.title: Conversation Pattern Library
projects.tagline: A searchable shelf of conversation patterns for when “just add chat” is not a design strategy.
projects.problem: Teams keep rediscovering the same conversational moves — clarify an ambiguous request, reveal complexity gradually, recover context, confirm a risky action — and then bury the reasoning inside individual mockups.
projects.portfolio_signal: This is the smallest project in the shortlist, but it shows a habit I care about: turning repeated design judgement into a reusable system without sanding away the context that made the pattern useful.
evidence: Six authored patterns span clarification, disclosure, error handling, handoff, and confirmation.
Every pattern includes when-to-use guidance, principles, four anatomy steps, a conversation example, and tags.
Search matches names, descriptions, and tags; category filters update the visible library count.
Live Preview, Anatomy, and Guidelines views let the same pattern be read as an example, a structure, or practical advice.
limitations: The library currently contains six patterns, so it is a convincing seed rather than a comprehensive system.
Patterns are static TypeScript data with no authoring, review, or contribution workflow.
There are no deep links to a selected pattern, and browser history does not capture exploration.
The composability note is promising, but related patterns are not yet modelled or navigable.
projects.learning: A pattern library is more than a gallery of good examples. It becomes useful when it explains selection, boundaries, and relationships. This version handles the first two; the next interesting job is making patterns compose.
projects.story:
I did not want to make another collection of polished chat bubbles. The more useful question was whether a designer could find the right conversational move for a particular moment, understand why it fits, and see how it unfolds over several turns.

The library currently holds six patterns: Clarification Loop, Progressive Disclosure, Graceful Fallback, Escalation to Human, Destructive Action Confirmation, and Context Recovery. Each uses the same schema, so search, category filters, anatomy diagrams, guidelines, and replayable examples all come from one source of truth.

It is intentionally a seed. Six patterns do not make a full design system, and the contribution model exists only in my head. But building the browser exposed the real next question: patterns should link to one another, because good conversations rarely use only one.
capabilities: Search patterns by name, description, or tags
Filter across five conversation-design categories
Browse a compact, collapsible pattern index
Switch between Live Preview, Anatomy, and Guidelines
Replay multi-turn examples with agent, user, and system roles
Inspect principles, tags, and when-to-use guidance

projects.title: Figma Redline Bridge
projects.tagline: A local bridge that lets an AI or script draw measurements, specs, and annotation batches inside Figma.
projects.problem: An external agent can reason about a design, but Figma plugins live in a sandbox and wait for a person to click things. I wanted a safe, inspectable bridge between those two worlds — mostly so I could stop drawing the same spacing arrows by hand.
projects.portfolio_signal: This project connects design judgement to working infrastructure. It is not an AI wrapper around a button; it is a small protocol, relay, plugin runtime, and set of Figma-native annotation primitives that have to agree with one another.
evidence: A four-hop command path connects HTTP clients to a Node relay, WebSocket, plugin iframe, and Figma sandbox.
Remote commands cover spacing, dimensions, colour, typography, page creation, and ordered batches.
The server queues commands while Figma is disconnected and exposes connection state through a health endpoint.
The plugin preserves auto-layout by rescaling cloned frames rather than mutating dimensions directly.
limitations: The localhost command endpoint is unauthenticated and should not be exposed beyond a trusted local machine.
Only one active Figma client is supported; a new connection replaces the previous socket.
Batch commands fail when Figma is disconnected instead of joining the normal pending-command queue.
Setup still requires a server process, a plugin build, and a manually loaded Figma development manifest.
There is no automated test suite for the cross-runtime message path.
projects.learning: The hard part was not drawing a line. It was respecting three different runtimes, Figma's plugin sandbox, and auto-layout without making the tool feel haunted. A boring protocol turned out to be the useful bit.
projects.story:
The first version answered a very small question: could I ask an external tool to draw a spacing redline in the Figma file I already had open? Figma's sandbox makes the answer more interesting than it sounds.

I ended up with a local relay. An HTTP request becomes a WebSocket message, crosses the plugin iframe through postMessage, and finally reaches the Figma API. Replies make the same trip back. On top of that plumbing I added spacing, dimension, colour, typography, page, and batch commands.

The project later grew into broader documentation experiments, but the bridge is still the part I like most. It automates repetition without claiming to automate taste. The person still decides what deserves an annotation; the tool just saves the small ceremony of drawing it.
capabilities: Send structured commands from an AI, CLI, or local script into Figma
Draw spacing measurements and dimension labels
Generate colour swatches and typography specification boxes
Create pages and execute ordered command batches
Queue pending commands until the plugin connects
Inspect server and Figma connection state through a health endpoint

projects.title: Figma MCP (Misnamed Experiment)
projects.tagline: A folder named figma-mcp that contains neither a Figma integration nor an MCP server. Naturally, I kept it.
projects.story: I created the folder expecting to connect Figma to MCP, then wandered off and built an early Owly marketing page instead. The result is a normal Next.js site with a hero, scroll highlights, a carousel, feature sections, and a before-and-after comparison. It is a different art direction from the site that eventually shipped. I kept the name because project archaeology deserves evidence too.
capabilities: Animated marketing homepage
Navigation + hero section
Scroll-highlight section
See it in action product demo section
Tabbed video carousel
Feature workflow section
Before/after comparison section
Reusable button/logo components

projects.title: FlowSense
projects.tagline: A screen-recording reviewer that turns a user flow into frame-level evidence, scores, and a cautious quality gate.
projects.problem: A screenshot can show that a screen looks off. It cannot show whether feedback arrived late, a user backtracked, or an error quietly swallowed the flow. Manual review can catch those things, but it takes time and varies with the reviewer.
projects.portfolio_signal: This is the most complete example of how I like to work: start with a fuzzy UX judgement, turn it into observable criteria, and build enough system around the rubric that the result can be inspected, challenged, and repeated.
evidence: A seven-category weighted rubric produces a 0–100 score and a pass, warn, or block quality gate.
The processor extracts video frames, selects meaningful changes, and separates fact extraction from rubric scoring.
Reports link issues and recommendations back to source frames, then compare repeat runs for regressions.
Signed webhooks, nonce replay protection, managed identity, and atomic SQL claims harden the processing path.
The monorepo includes targeted tests, CI gates, shared schemas, and an evaluation harness for agreement and release metrics.
limitations: It depends on configured Azure services and credentials, so it is not a one-click public demo.
Authentication is deliberately bypassed in the local-development path and must be re-enabled for deployment.
The benchmark machinery exists, but the repository does not include a populated human-labelled holdout set.
Long recordings can hit the analysis budget; when that happens the system downgrades the quality gate instead of bluffing.
The model can surface evidence and apply a rubric, but a designer still has to judge whether the rubric fits the product.
projects.learning: The biggest improvements came from structuring the evidence, not simply swapping models. Separating “what happened?” from “how well did it happen?” made the analysis easier to reason about — and easier to distrust in useful ways.
projects.story:
FlowSense began with a design question: could a recorded user flow be reviewed against a consistent rubric without pretending the score was objective truth? I wanted the speed of automation, but not the false confidence of a mysterious number.

The project grew from a local model experiment into a monorepo with an upload interface, video processor, shared schemas, storage, frame analysis, and a report. The useful architectural shift was two-pass inference: first describe the observable interaction facts, then score those facts against the rubric. “What happened?” and “Was it good?” should not be the same prompt.

I kept adding places for the system to show its work. Issues point back to frames. Repeat runs show regressions. Truncated analysis can warn but not pass. The result is not a replacement for design review; it is a way to arrive at review with a consistent first pass and a trail of receipts.
capabilities: Upload and process UX screen recordings
Extract frames and select meaningful interaction changes
Apply a seven-category rubric through two-pass vision analysis
Surface quality gates, issue evidence, recommendations, and regression deltas
Navigate from report findings back to source frames
Store runs and media with Azure SQL and Blob Storage
Protect processor calls with signed webhooks and replay checks
Export review reports as PDF

projects.title: Owly Studio
projects.tagline: The working product behind Owly: part creative workspace, part editor, part ongoing lesson in scope.
projects.story: Owly Studio is where the company idea has to survive contact with buttons, loading states, auth, real media, and people who do not share our internal vocabulary. I have worked across onboarding, the creator dashboard, libraries, forms, and the timeline editor, with Supabase, FastAPI, and Remotion underneath. The recurring design question is how to make the product more capable without turning every screen into a cockpit. Hundreds of commits later, the answer is still moving.
capabilities: Supabase email/Google auth
Onboarding flow
AI chat-style creator dashboard
Video/storyboard/static ad library
Timeline editor with Remotion player
Static ad creation and editing
UGC and CGI video forms
Brand DNA / product catalog tools
Featured videos explore page
Admin and profile pages

projects.title: Owly Marketing Site
projects.tagline: The public face of Owly, where motion tells the story and the product still has to earn the click.
projects.story: The Owly website had a different job from the product: explain the idea quickly enough that someone wanted to see more. I gave it a lighter, more cinematic rhythm with a video-backed hero, motion, feature stories, and product moments. It is mostly a presentation layer, but that does not make the design easy — every flourish still has to clarify the product rather than distract from it.
capabilities: Landing page with video hero
About page
Features page
Pricing page
Blog index and article pages
Tool pages
Learn pages
Privacy and terms pages
Animated UI with Framer Motion/GSAP
MDX/content-driven pages
